params.text:
A balanced diet and the Eatwell Guide

The NHS Eatwell Guide shows the balance of foods that make up a healthy diet over a day or week. It divides food into groups and shows roughly how much of each we should aim for:

  • Fruit and vegetables: aim for at least five portions a day.
  • Starchy carbohydrates: potatoes, bread, rice and pasta, choosing wholegrain where possible.
  • Protein: beans, pulses, fish, eggs and meat.
  • Dairy or alternatives: milk, cheese and yoghurt or fortified substitutes.
  • Oils and spreads: only small amounts, and limit foods high in fat, salt and sugar.

Why nutrients matter in later life

Each nutrient does a job. Protein helps maintain muscle and heal wounds, calcium and vitamin D keep bones strong, fibre supports digestion, and iron and vitamins keep the body working well. Older and unwell people often eat smaller amounts, so every mouthful needs to count.

Appetite can fade with age, illness or medication, and small, frequent, nourishing meals often work better than large plates. Remember that the Eatwell Guide is aimed at healthy adults; someone who is underweight or at risk of malnutrition may need a higher-calorie, fortified approach set out in their care plan rather than a low-fat one.

params.question: The standard Eatwell Guide balance of foods is the right target for everyone, including people who are underweight or at risk of malnutrition.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. This is false: someone who is underweight or malnourished may need a fortified, higher-calorie diet rather than the standard Eatwell balance.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this is false. The Eatwell Guide is designed for the general healthy population. People who are underweight or malnourished often need a higher-calorie, fortified diet set out in their care plan.
